title: TEAP (Tunnel EAP) Authentication Mode Tracking and Inner-Method Failure Detection

compliance.assurance_rationale: IA-2(1) MFA — TEAP supports user+machine joint authentication.
PCI 8.3.1 strong authentication — TEAP joint cert+credential.
AC.L2-3.1.1 — TEAP enforces strong device+user authentication.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Disable user cert on TEAP supplicant. Inner-method failures spike. UC-17.1.69 fires.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Working TEAP. Low failure rate; chaining result mostly 'user-and-machine'.

dataSources: ISE syslog `index=nac sourcetype=cisco:ise:syslog` *Authentication* category. TEAP (RFC 7170) is Cisco's recommended EAP method for joint user+machine authentication. Authentication events with `EapAuthentication=EAP-TEAP` carry inner-method fields `EapTunnel`, `EapInnerMethod` (e.g. EAP-TLS, MS-CHAPv2). Failure codes `5400`, `5440`, `5417` are extended with TEAP-specific reasons in the `FailureReason` field.
spl:
index=nac sourcetype="cisco:ise:syslog" EapAuthentication="EAP-TEAP"
| stats count, count(eval(MessageCode=="5400")) as failures by EapInnerMethod, EapChainingResult
| where failures > 50
description: Tracks the share of TEAP authentications versus other EAP methods, decomposes TEAP into its inner methods (EAP-TLS, MS-CHAPv2), and detects high failure rates per inner method or per chaining result (user-only vs. machine-only vs. both).
value: TEAP enables true user+machine attestation for Zero Trust. Tracking adoption shows progress; tracking failure rates flags integration issues (machine cert problems, user identity store failures, switch-side TEAP support gaps).
implementation: Schedule every 1h. TEAP requires Windows 11 22H2+ or Cisco Secure Client native, plus switch IOS-XE 17.x+ supplicant and ISE 3.0+ server.
visualization: (1) TEAP adoption percentage trend. (2) Inner-method failure breakdown. (3) Chaining-result distribution (user-and-machine vs. user-only vs. machine-only).
knownFalsePositives:
(a) Initial TEAP rollout — high failures during supplicant config drift.
(b) Machine cert renewal cycles cause temporary failures.
mitreAttack: N/A (authentication hygiene — measures TEAP/EAP-chained authentication coverage, no adversary technique)
grandmaExplanation: TEAP authenticates both the device and the user at the same time — like requiring both a building badge and an ID. We watch how often this works versus fails to make sure the strong authentication is rolling out smoothly.
